headline: Lewis and Clark Cross Continental Divide, 1806
body:
The vast, untamed heart of the continent, a land of soaring peaks and hidden valleys, yielded its secrets grudgingly to the intrepid spirits who dared to penetrate its depths. On that seventh day of July, in the year of our Lord eighteen hundred and six, Captain William Clark, with his resolute band, pressed onward, a mere fragment of the grand expedition, yet burdened with a mission of profound import: to chart the course of the Yellowstone, a river rumored to be as wild and untamed as the land itself. The morning dawned with a vexing challenge, their horses, those indispensable companions of the wilderness journey, scattered across the expansive plains. Men were dispatched in every direction, their keen eyes scanning the horizon for any sign of the errant beasts. By six o'clock, all but nine were accounted for, their absence a gnawing worry in the vast solitude. Clark, ever the pragmatist, ordered a wider search, yet by ten, the men returned, their efforts fruitless, convinced that the cunning Shoshones had spirited away their most valuable mounts under the cloak of night. 

Undeterred, Clark made the difficult decision to leave a small party to continue the search, while he, with the main body, pushed towards the canoes, intending to expose them to the sun's drying embrace. At half past ten, they resumed their march, traversing an open, fertile valley, a verdant carpet woven with the threads of four great creeks, their bottoms wide and often mired. The landscape unfolded before them, a testament to the ceaseless sculpting power of water and ice. After sixteen miles, a marvel appeared, a Boiling Spring, situated a hundred paces from a large easterly fork of a small river. Clark, with a scientist's curiosity and a frontiersman's practicality, observed its peculiar nature: "this Spring [NB: 15 yds in circumc, boils up all over bottom which is Stoney] contains a very considerable quantity of water, and actually blubbers with heat for 20 paces below where it rises. it has every appearance of boiling, too hot for a man to endure his hand in it 3 seconds." He directed Sergeant Pryor and John Shields to test its efficacy, placing pieces of meat within its scalding embrace, one cooked in twenty-five minutes, the other, thicker, in thirty-two. The water, slightly sulfurous, bubbled forth from loose, gritty stone, a subterranean furnace revealing itself to the open sky. 

After a brief repast and allowing their horses to graze, they continued, crossing the easterly branch and ascending the north side of the middle fork. Nine miles further, they crossed it again near the head of an easterly branch, finally passing through a gap in the mountains. Here, amidst beautiful springs that fed into Willards Creek, they made camp, the vastness of the land stretching out around them, a silent, majestic witness to their relentless journey. The air, crisp and clear, carried the scent of pine and damp earth, a symphony of the wild. The immense beaver signs spoke of a thriving ecosystem, a world teeming with life, largely untouched by the hand of man. As the moon ascended, casting long shadows across the rugged terrain, the sentinel stood watch, a solitary figure against the boundless expanse, guarding the dreams of a young nation pushing westward, ever westward, into the heart of the unknown. The Continental Divide, a silent, ancient barrier, had been breached, another thread woven into the grand tapestry of exploration.
